title: Dynamic Programming Intro in Java
summary: Overlapping subproblems, optimal substructure, memoization (top-down) vs tabulation (bottom-up), Fibonacci, climbing stairs, coin change.

questions.prompt: Dynamic Programming is applicable when a problem has:
questions.explanation: Both properties must hold: subproblems repeat AND the global optimum can be built from subproblem optima.

questions.prompt: Overlapping subproblems means:
choices: The problem has duplicate input data
The recursion tree computes the same inputs multiple times
The problem has multiple valid answers
questions.explanation: Naive Fibonacci computes fib(2) an exponential number of times — that is the overlap.

questions.prompt: Memoization transforms naive Fibonacci from O(2^n) to O(n) because:
choices: Each of the n unique sub-problems is solved exactly once and cached
questions.explanation: Cache lookup is O(1) — every unique fib(k) is computed once, giving n unique computations.

questions.prompt: Tabulated Fibonacci dp[] for n=5:
Tabulation (bottom-up) advantage over memoization (top-down):
choices: No recursion overhead or call-stack risk; easier to apply space optimisation
questions.explanation: Iterative table fills with no call-stack depth; also you can reduce space once old states are no longer needed.

questions.prompt: Space-optimised Fibonacci uses O(1) space instead of O(n) by:
choices: Keeping only the last two computed values
questions.explanation: dp[i] depends only on dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] — two variables suffice.

questions.prompt: Climbing stairs (1 or 2 steps) recurrence is:
questions.explanation: At stair i you arrived from i-1 or i-2 — total ways = sum of ways to reach those two stairs.

questions.prompt: Climbing stairs n=4. Answer:
Coin change dp[] initial fill value should be:
choices: amount + 1 (a safe infinity beyond any valid answer)
questions.explanation: Using amount+1 avoids overflow when doing min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1). Valid answer is always <= amount.

questions.prompt: Coin change inner loop iterates over:
choices: All coins for each amount i
All amounts for each coin (also valid — same result)
questions.explanation: For each target amount i, try every coin — pick the one that gives the fewest total coins.

questions.prompt: Optimal substructure means:
choices: The problem can be split into independent subproblems
The optimal solution to the big problem contains optimal solutions to its subproblems
All subproblems have the same size
questions.explanation: The coin change optimum for amount 11 builds on the optimum for amount 11-coin — that is optimal substructure.

questions.prompt: A memo sentinel value of -1 is UNSAFE when:
choices: -1 is a valid answer to a subproblem
questions.explanation: If a subproblem can genuinely return -1, using -1 as sentinel causes you to recompute it unnecessarily.

questions.prompt: 0/1 knapsack differs from coin change in that each item:
choices: Can be used UNLIMITED times (coin change) vs exactly ONCE (0/1 knapsack)
questions.explanation: Unbounded: inner loop can reuse same coin. 0/1: must iterate items in outer loop and capacity backward.

questions.prompt: Which are valid 1D DP problems?
questions.explanation: All-pairs shortest paths (Floyd-Warshall) is 2D DP. The others use a single 1D array.

questions.prompt: The 'state' in a DP problem represents:
choices: All information needed to compute the answer from this point forward without looking backward
questions.explanation: State definition is the hardest part of DP. Example: (index, remaining capacity) for knapsack.

questions.prompt: House robber: dp[i] = max money from first i houses, can't rob adjacent. Recurrence:
questions.explanation: At house i: either skip it (dp[i-1]) or rob it (dp[i-2] + nums[i]).

questions.prompt: House robber on [2,7,9,3,1]. Max:
Top-down DP call stack depth for fib(1000) could cause:
questions.explanation: JVM default stack handles ~5000–10000 frames. fib(1000) is fine, but fib(100000) could fail. Tabulation avoids this.

questions.prompt: 'Can I reach the last index?' (Jump Game) uses DP where dp[i] means:
choices: Whether index i is reachable from index 0
questions.explanation: dp[i] = true if any previous reachable index j had jump >= i-j.

questions.prompt: Greedy works for some problems where DP does — when should you prefer greedy?
choices: When a locally optimal choice provably leads to a global optimum (e.g. coin change with canonical denominations)
questions.explanation: Greedy coin change works for {1,5,10,25} but fails for arbitrary denominations — DP is the safe general solution.

flashcards.back: 1. Overlapping subproblems (same inputs recomputed). 2. Optimal substructure (big optimum built from sub-optima).
Recurse normally; cache result before returning. Uses call stack + memo array.
Fill a dp[] table from base cases up. No recursion. Easier to space-optimise.
When dp[i] only needs dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], use two variables instead of array.
dp[0]=0, dp[i]=min(dp[i-coin]+1) for all coins. Init dp[1..n] = amount+1 (infinity).
dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Same as Fibonacci. dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2.
All info needed to solve a subproblem without looking backward. Hardest DP step.
0/1: each item used once (iterate capacity backward). Unbounded: reuse allowed (forward).

project.brief:
Write DPIntroLab.java with:
1. fibonacci(int n) — tabulation, O(n) time O(1) space
2. climbStairs(int n) — tabulation O(1) space
3. coinChange(int[] coins, int amount) → int  (-1 if impossible)
4. rob(int[] nums) → int  (house robber)
5. canJump(int[] nums) → boolean  (Jump Game — greedy DP)
Include main() tests: coin change impossible case, house robber single element, jump game with zero gap.

checklist.text: fibonacci uses only two variables, no array
climbStairs correctly handles n=1 and n=2 base cases
coinChange initialises dp[1..amount] to amount+1 (not MAX_VALUE)
rob handles empty array and single-element array
canJump tracks maxReach and returns false if i > maxReach

hints: coinChange: if dp[amount] > amount after filling, return -1.
rob: iterate with two variables prev2, prev1 for O(1) space.
canJump: maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]). If i > maxReach at any point, return false.
